narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel incredibly disheartening amid the unique challenges you may have faced. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 school decile of 4, Year 3 decile of 3, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—against patterns from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam. It predicted a 68.8% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's denial, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed and pass on the first attempt. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 3, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile of 4, also lower than the average 5.71, which reinforced concerns about your overall undergraduate standing relative to others who passed the bar. Your upper-middle family income played a similar role, as the model associates certain income brackets with varying bar success rates in the dataset, nudging the prediction toward failure here. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36, close to the 36.7 average, strongly pulled in the opposite direction, suggesting solid potential and aligning well with successful students. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was nearly average at 3.21 but still tipped slightly toward risk, while your full-time status offered a small positive boost, matching the norm for 92.7% of students. Overall, your profile showed strengths like that LSAT score but was outweighed by those lower decile rankings, placing you below typical bar-passing students in key academic metrics. This isn't a reflection of your worth or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers—it's just one model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, preparing rigorously for a higher LSAT if possible, and seeking mentorship from bar-passers who share your background; many have turned similar predictions around through targeted improvement. You're capable of rewriting this story—keep pushing.
